title: Text with holes
checkLabels.t8: The line break is not manual
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The code works. The task is to make it readable.
why.not-closed: A tag in index.html is open and never closed.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: A backtick is not a regular quote and not an apostrophe. It sits under the Esc key on most keyboards, to the left of the number one.
why.still-glued: You are still joining with plus.
hint2.still-glued: Both lines get rewritten, not just the first one.
why.no-template: You are not using template strings.
hint2.no-template: Regular quotes understand nothing. You need a different mark — the backtick, to the left of the number one.
hint3.no-template: Between backticks everything is text, except what sits inside a dollar sign and curly braces. That is where a name or a sum goes.
hint4.no-template: Swap the double quotes for backticks, remove the pluses, and wrap each name in a dollar sign with curly braces.
why.manual-newline: You are still adding line breaks by hand.
hint2.manual-newline: Template strings remember a pressed Enter. Just press Enter inside them.
why.wrong-line: The line does not come out right.
hint2.wrong-line: It should read: Bean Street sold 40 cups for 420 lv. Check the spaces — they are part of the text.
why.wrong-receipt: The receipt does not come out right.
hint2.wrong-receipt: Three lines: Shop, Cups, Total. Each on its own line, each with a colon and a space after it.
why.wrong-count: There are not two lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: The code works. Both lines come out correctly in the console.
blocks.1.text: But read them. The first is seven pluses and eight quote marks for a single sentence. The second is worse — it also contains something called backslash n, which you have to remember means a new line.
blocks.2.text: The problem is not that it fails
blocks.3.text: The problem is that it cannot be read. To work out what comes out, you have to assemble the sentence in your head, piece by piece, skipping over the quotes and pluses.
blocks.4.text: And every new piece is a new chance to forget a space. A missing space at the end of a piece does not break anything — two words simply stick together.
blocks.6.text: There is a third kind of quote mark. Not double, not single, but a backtick. It sits to the left of the number one, under Esc.
blocks.7.text: Between them text is just text — but whatever you put inside a dollar sign and curly braces is worked out and dropped into place.
blocks.8.text: You write the sentence the way it reads. The holes are where the values go.
blocks.10.text: Regular quotes do not allow a pressed Enter — the code breaks. That is why backslash n exists, meaning a new line.
blocks.11.text: Template strings do allow it. Press Enter inside one and it stays. The text in the code looks exactly like the text that comes out.
blocks.13.text: Rewrite both lines with template strings. The result must be exactly the same — this is an exercise in readability, not behaviour.
blocks.14.text: The receipt should be three lines, but without backslash n. Press Enter inside the string.
